description: Active Inference Ontology terms (v5, 2023-05-25), sourced from github.com/ActiveInferenceInstitute/Active_Inference_Ontology (Ontology_v5_May_25_2023.csv). Definitions, examples, and connections are public.

terms.definition: Broad sense: how “close to the mark” an Estimator is.
terms.definition2: Narrow sense: the expected or realized extent of Surprise on an estimation, usually about Sense State reflecting theRecognition density
terms.examples: A lower-resolution camera or fMRI has lower Accuracy and thus lesser capacity to map fine scale features of stimuli.
terms.connections: Accuracy is the inverse of Uncertainty

terms.definition: Broad sense: Extent to which stimuli have multiple plausible interpretations, requiring priors &/or Action for disambiguation
terms.definition2: Narrow sense: Specific model parameter used to model Uncertainty, usually about sensory Perception .
terms.examples: The noisy readings from the thermometer resulted in high Ambiguity given the Sensory input to the Agent .
terms.connections: Uncertainty on Hidden State givenSensory input

terms.definition: As opposed to frequentist analysis, Bayesian Inference uses a specified Prior or Empirical prior to Update the distributional Posterior
terms.examples: Many of the key ideas of Bayesian Inference existed before Rev. Bayes, and in some cases reflect recent contributions from computational research.
terms.connections: As a matter of determining the ratio of populations to sample, the following is instructive > https://aeon.co/videos/what-is-it-to-be-bayesian-the-pretty-simple-math-modelling-behind-a-big-data-buzzword

terms.definition: Broad sense: Felt sense by an Agent of something being true, or confidence it is the case.
terms.definition2: Narrow sense: the State of a Random variable in a Bayesian Inference scheme.
terms.examples: The Prior or Empirical prior in Bayesian Inference can be a Belief on Hidden State .
terms.connections: A Belief is the most likely Hypothesis that provides an Explanation for the cause of Sense State, where a cause here is a Hidden State of the Generative Process.

terms.definition: Belief updating is changes in a Bayesian Inference Belief through time.
terms.examples: The incoming Sensory Data resulted in Belief updating
terms.connections: In belief updating the Prior is altered to reflect incoporation of new Evidence presented to the model from the Generative Process.

terms.definition: The extent to which an Agent must revise a Belief to explain incoming Sensory observations.
terms.definition2: The Kullback-Leibler Divergence between the Prior and Posterior which is used in Bayesian model selection to find the simplest (least complex) model and avoid overfitting on the noise inherent in Sensory observations.
terms.examples: Model Complexity can refer to the number of predictor or independent variables or features that a model needs to take into account in order to make accurate prediction.

terms.definition: Data are a set of values of qualitative or quantitative variables about one or more Agent or object .
terms.examples: These three readings from the thermometer constitute Data!
terms.connections: Evidence is Data that is evaluated by Agent in the context of their Generative Model

terms.definition: Within a Bayesian Inference framework, Expectation is an Estimator about future timesteps
terms.examples: At timestep 1, the Agent made a prediction about Expected Free Energy through time, this was an Expectation about the future.
terms.connections: Anticipation, Prediction, Prediction error .

terms.definition: Process of reaching a (local or global) conclusion within a Model, for example with Bayesian Inference.
terms.definition2: The process of using a Sensory observation (observed variable, data) along with a known set of parameters to determine the state of an unknown, Latent cause (unobserved variable).
terms.examples: The researcher made Model of Active Vision where the Agent was doing Inference on Action (Action Planning , Action Prediction ) as well as Perception (perceptual inference ).
terms.connections: Action (more generally) and perceptual inference and Bayesian Inference (more specifically).

terms.definition: Measured in bits, the reduction of Uncertainty on a Belief distribution of some type. Usually Syntactic (Shannon) but also can be Semantic (e.g. Bayesian ).
terms.examples: There is more maximum Information in 1 terabyte than in 1 gigabyte.

terms.definition: Broad sense: Process of an Agent engaged in Updates to Cognition (and possibly) Behavior.
terms.definition2: Narrow sense: Process of Bayesian Inference where Generative Model parameters undergo Belief updating
terms.examples: The software agent engaged in Belief updating on internal parameters, this is technically Learning .
terms.connections: Learning model parameters is called parameter estimation and is obtained by using Sensory observations and previously inferred Hidden State. In Bayesian Inference parameters are treated as Random variables.

terms.definition: If we consider the environment as a Generative Process that can be sampled when in a particular state, the statistical result (Data, Sensory observation) of the sampling is known as the outcome.
terms.definition2: The Data produced by sampling a Generative Process (a joint distribution on states and outcomes).
terms.examples: The Generative Process produces outcomes when we sample from it.
terms.connections: Generative modelling involves using outcomes to approximate a statistical model of the Generative Process.

terms.definition: The Update to the Prior after Observation has occurred
terms.definition2: In Bayes’ theorem, the Posterior is equal to the product of the Likelihood and Prior divided by the model evidence.
terms.examples: The Posterior distribution reflects our degrees of Belief about Latent causes after we see Sensory Data.
terms.connections: Solving an Inverse problem to go from Outcomes given a State determining a State given an Outcome. In representation learning the Posterior is called the Recognition Model since it “recognizes” the Latent cause that underlie the generation of the Sensory outcomes.

terms.definition: An Estimator about a State in a Model at a future time.
terms.definition2: The process of using a learned Generative Model to forecast what value a future Hidden State will be.
terms.examples: After successfully Learning the structure of the Generative Process the Agent can make a Prediction about the future state of this Generative Process and the associated Sensory Data it will generate.
terms.connections: The top-down priors aim at Prediction the incoming Sensory Data based on what the Agent has learned about the environment. An incorrect Prediction results in a Prediction error which the Agent attempts to minimize in the long run in the process of Prediction error minimization.

terms.definition: The initial or preceding state of a Belief in Bayesian Inference, before Sensory Data (Observation or Evidence ) occurs.
terms.examples: Active Vision uses Prior on Sensory input .
terms.connections: In the Active Inference (Continuous state space formulation), the Prior is represented by a Stochastic State space equation which represents the Agent‘s belief about how the Hidden State in the environment change over time.

terms.definition: is the statistical, computational, or mathematical value for a parameter within the State space of a Model .
terms.examples: Blanket State is a type of State that partition Internal State from External State
terms.connections: 111. Situation, Condition, State (from here) The words, situation and condition, imply, something accidental, and transitory; with this difference, that situation, respects outward circumstances; condition, those, within the matter referred to; whereas, that of state,implies, something, more habitual, and lasting. We, generally, use the word, situation, as relative, to affairs, rank or fortune; condition, with respect, to the nature, quality or property of a thing; and that, of state, applying it to health, or, our well or ill-being. We say, our situation is bad; when we are surrounded with difficulties: that a building is in bad condition; when out of repair; and, that some persons enjoy but an indifferent state of health. An ill-state of health, added to a bad situation of affairs, is a condition, into which, every man must, naturally, dread the falling. Such is the condition, and vicissitude of human life, that, the most prudent men, have, often, found themselves in perplex|ing situations; and from a state of happiness, have, as it were, through the perverseness of fortune, fallen into one most wretched and deplorable.

terms.definition: Set of variables/parameters that describe a System .
terms.definition2: A state space is the set of all possible configurations of a system
terms.examples: The State space of a Generative Model can be a Continuous state space or Discrete state space .

terms.definition: Of a Random variable , that it is described by parameters that are drawn from a Gaussian distribution and unchanging over the time horizon of analysis.
terms.examples: A common assumption of many time series algorithms is that the data exhibits Stationarity.
terms.connections: A common assumption in many time series techniques is that the data are at Stationarity . A stationary process has the property that the mean, variance and autocorrelation structure do not change over time. Stationarity can be defined in precise mathematical terms, but for our purpose we mean a flat looking series, without trend, constant variance over time, a constant autocorrelation structure over time and no periodic fluctuations (seasonality). For practical purposes, stationarity can usually be determined from a run sequence plot. https://www.itl.nist.gov/div898/handbook/pmc/section4/pmc442.htm

terms.definition: In Bayesian Inference, Surprise is the negative log evidence, directly corresponding to the inverse of probability (high probability, low surprisal; low probability, high surprisal). The proxy that bounds surprisal is the difference between Prior and Posterior Distribution — how “surprising” Sensory Data are to the Generative Model of the Agent.
terms.definition2: Surprise (also known as Surprisal or self-Information), is a quantity that, according to the Free Energy Principle, must be minimized in order for an Agent to survive; Variational Free Energy provides an upper bound on Surprisal and is minimized instead of minimizing Surprisal directly.
terms.examples: Surprisal cannot be minimized directly because it is involves calculating the Evidence term in Bayes’ Rule which generally involves an intractable integral over all possible states an organism can be in.
terms.connections: An organism that minimizes Surprisal can maintain Non-Equilibrium Steady State and will consistently revisit an Attracting set of states. Minimizing Surprisal is equivalent to maximizing model evidence.

terms.definition: The length of a time window or horizon considered (longer time → deeper / more Temporal Depth )
terms.examples: In deep temporal models, Temporal Depth or occurs because of the number of Counterfactual possibilities one must account for increases as more future states are modeled (see: https://www.frontiersin.org/articles/10.3389/fpsyg.2018.00579/full)
terms.connections: From Breaking down unitization: Is the whole greater than the sum of its parts? “Depth” as a measured difference applied to change (time passing) as Memory and Cognition . “Graf and Schacter (1989) first described unitization as the process whereby multiple, separate, items become represented as a single unit, either through perceiving or conceiving of a structure (e.g., semantic meaning) that would connect the disparate units.” Which leaves us with at least two ways of “structuring” time units (episodes and cycles (diachronic)).

terms.definition: In Bayesian Inference , a measure of the Expectation of Surprise (Entropy) of a Random variable (associated with its variance or inverse Precision )
terms.definition2: Random fluctuations around the true value of a quantity we are trying to measure that are a result of uncontrolled variables in the environment or measurement apparatus.
terms.examples: A measure of unpredictability or expected Surprise (cf, Entropy ). The Uncertainty about a Random variable is often quantified with its Variance (inverse Precision ).
terms.connections: From Basics of Estimating Measurement Uncertainty https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2556585/There is a table of terminology of the science of measurement (metrology) that includes common metrological terms and their definitions, all of which recognize that making measurement comparisons leads to some degree of measurement uncertainty.

terms.definition: Broad sense: The dynamics, mechanisms, and measurements of Behavior
terms.definition2: Narrow sense: The sequence of Active States enacted by an Agent via Policy selection from Affordance
terms.examples: Ants are continually involved in Action in real life (Realism) and/or in a Model (Instrumentalism)
terms.connections: Action of the Agent is selected by Action Planning . As the consequences of Action are not fully known, the Expected Free Energy is calculated over Affordance and used for Variational Free Energy Action and Planning as Divergence Minimization

terms.definition: The selection of an Affordance based upon Inference of Expected Free Energy
terms.examples: The robot assessed its current and target location, then engaged in Action Planning to decide under time pressure how to navigate.
terms.connections: Action Planning draws from set of Affordances and uses Expected Free Energy Inference, also known as Action Prediction .

terms.definition: The ability of an Agent to engage in Action in their Niche and enact Goal-driven selection or Policy selection based upon Preference
terms.examples: An Agent uses their Agency to sculpt their environment which makes it more predictable thereby minimizing the agent’sVariational Free Energy and maintaining its Markov Blanket in the face of random fluctuations.
terms.connections: Agent has Agency

terms.definition: The sequence of Action that an Agent is observed to enact.
terms.examples: I expect you to be on your best Behavior! The Active Inference Agent uses its selected Policy to take Action that we call its Behavior.

terms.definition: Sequence of Actions, reflected by series of Active States as implemented in Policy selection which is Action Prediction or Action and Planning as Divergence Minimization
terms.examples: The sequence of Actions that an Agent plans to take is a Policy.
terms.connections: Policy selection in Active Inference is about Prediction error minimization or minimization of Expected Free Energy

terms.definition: The process of an Agent engaging in Action Planning from set of Affordance, in Active Inference based upon minimization of Expected Free Energy
terms.examples: When the Agent decided to go one way instead of the other, it was due to an internal process of Policy selection, specifically Action and Planning as Divergence Minimization.
terms.connections: Similar to Decision-making

terms.definition: parameter in Bayesian Inference Markov Decision Process that ranks or scores the extent to how an Agent values Sensory input .
terms.examples: The Generative Model of the bacteria underwent parameter fitting (Belief updating / Learning ) on Action , guided by a Preference for medium but not high/low sugar concentration.
terms.connections: In the Discrete state space formulation of Active Inference, the Preferences or habits of an Agent can be encapsulated in a Prior Preference Distribution, a target distribution of States the agent wishes to end up due to its phenotype and other habits it has picked up throughout its existence.

terms.definition: Active Inference is a Process Theory related to Free Energy Principle .
terms.examples: It is fun and rewarding to learn and apply Active Inference as a framework for Perception, Cognition , and Action — it is a Process Theory compatible with the Free Energy Principle.
terms.connections: The conclusion of Active Inference is a Hypothesis (conjecture) about the feasibility of adopting certain Policy (strategy profiles) that the Agent makes a Generative Model with Temporal Depth.

terms.definition: is the value of Information gain or Expectation of reduction in Uncertainty about a State with respect to a Policy, used in Policy selection
terms.examples: When the environment contains Uncertainty I would like to undertake the action of Foraging for the sake of it to see if I can gather more relevant Observations to explain what is going on around me. Such relevant Observations have Epistemic value for me as an Agent.
terms.connections: It is variously known as Bayesian surprise, epistemic affordance, the value of information, intrinsic motivation and so on.

terms.definition: Measure for performing Inference on Action over a given time horizon (Policy selection , Action and Planning as Divergence Minimization ).
terms.definition2: The two components of Expected Free Energy are the imperative to satisfy Preferences, and the penalty for failing to minimize Expectation of Surprisal.
terms.examples: The deep affective Inference Agent used Expected Free Energy calculation as a basis of Policy selection .
terms.connections: Expected Free Energy can be seen as....

terms.definition: Free Energy is an Information Theoretic quantity that constitutes an upper bound on Surprisal .
terms.definition2: Free Energy can refer to various or multiple sub-types of Free Energy: * Variational Free Energy * Expected Free Energy * Free Energy of the Expected Future * Helmholtz Free Energy * ....
terms.examples: Free Energy can refer to various different formulations and decompositions, that share some important key features.

terms.definition: A generalization of Predictive Coding (PC) according to which organisms minimize an upper bound on the Entropy of Sensory input (or sensory signals) (the Free Energy). Under specific assumptions, Free Energy translates toPrediction error .
terms.definition2: A set of statistical principles that describe how Agents can maintain their self-organization in the face of random fluctuations from the environment.
terms.examples: As a principle, the Free Energy Principle cannot be falsified.
terms.connections: An Agent that is consistent with the Free Energy Principle will be able to minimize Variational Free Energy by taking Actions or using Perception in a way that maximizes the Evidence for its Generative Model of the environment. The Free Energy Principle is a more general statement about specific kinds of self-organization in random Non-linear dynamical systems assuming that these systems have certain properties like Ergodicity (weakly mixing) and a Markov Blanket. According to the Free Energy Principle, systems that minimize Variational Free Energy will maintain their low (thermodynamic) Entropy structure through Autopoiesis in the face of random fluctuations in the environment. To do so, they must take Actions that maintain the integrity of their Markov Blanket and develop a Generative Model that can successfully predict future states of the environment based on the Sensory Data they receive through the Markov Blanket.

terms.definition: Past Variational Free Energy plus future Expected Free Energy (each totaled over Policy).
terms.examples: Parr and Friston 2019 wrote “Crucially, this means the Generalized Free Energy reduces to the Variational Free Energy for outcomes that had been observed in the past.....Outcomes in the Generalized Free Energy formulation are represented explicitly as Beliefs. This means that the Prior over Sensory outcome is incorporated explicitly in the generative model.”

terms.definition: Pragmatic Value is the benefit to an organism of a given Policy or Action , measured in terms of probability of a Policy leading to Expectation of Random variable values that are aligned with the Preference of the Agent
terms.definition2: Pragmatic value describes the extent to which a given action is aligned with rewarding preferences over sensory outcomes.
terms.examples: The Agent had Preferences for there to be more beans in the jar, so adding more beans was of Pragmatic Value for them.

terms.definition: Measure that performs e.g. Inference on Sensory Data given a Generative Model , F. The two sources that composeVariational Free Energy are Model overfitting and Model accuracy
terms.examples: Variational Free Energy is a tractable way to compute an upper bound on Surprisal of a Generative Model given Data.

terms.definition: In the Friston Blanket formalism, the Blanket State are the Sense State (incoming Sensory input) and Active States (outgoing influence of Policy selection )
terms.examples: The Active States of the computer program are the statistical outputs that it presents, while the Sensory input Sense State are the incoming statistical dependencies.
terms.connections: Active States and Sense State make Blanket State, which partition Internal State and External State in the Markov Blanket partitioning formalism.

terms.definition: Set of states in the Markov Blanket Partition that make Internal State and External State have Conditional Probability that are independent.
terms.examples: I don’t care whether it is an Action or Sense State, as long as it is a Blanket State!

terms.definition: States with Conditional density independent from Internal State, conditioned on Blanket State.
terms.examples: In my Generative Model of temperature, the true temperature in the room is being modeled as an External State (Hidden State), so we will never know it.

terms.definition: Markov Blanket with partitioned Active States and Sense State .
terms.examples: A Friston Blanket is a type of Markov Blanket where Sense States are associated with incoming Sensory Data and Action states are associated with outgoing Behavior of the Agent.

terms.definition: States with Conditional density independent from External State , conditioned on Blanket State.
terms.examples: When the Generative Process describes a forest outside, and the Blanket States describe the bark of the tree, the Internal States describe the core of the tree.

terms.definition: Markov Partitioning Model of System, reflecting Agent as delineated from the Niche via an Interface. The Markov Blanket Blanket State reflect the State(s) upon which Internal State and External State are conditionally independent.
terms.examples: Markov Blankets are features of Maps (e.g. Bayesian Graphical Models), not of Territories (e.g. ant colonies or brains).

terms.definition: Bayesian Inference Model where Agent Generative Model can implement Policy selection on Affordances reflected by Active States, while other features of the Generative Process are outside the Control (states) of the Agent .
terms.examples: Whether it is fully observable or not, one common type of model used in control theory is a Markov Decision Process
terms.connections: Is Markov Blanket one way, and thus the Friston Blanket has x2 @markovboundaries linked to the decision making process. The probability and Preference Information Geometry between the x2 markov boundaries at each nested levels being updated gives the @temporality at the heart of @activeinference

terms.definition: In the Friston Blanket formalism, the Blanket State are the Sense State (incoming Sensory input) and Active States (outgoing influence of Policy selection )
terms.examples: In this model, the Observations coming in from the thermometers are considered as Sense States.

terms.definition: Options or capacities for Action by an Agent (sometimes called “Affordance 3.0”)
terms.definition2: From Ecological Psychology, the Perception of a possibility for Action (sometimes called “Affordance 2.0”).
terms.examples: “In this T-maze model, there are 3 Affordance for movement at the junction (Left, Right, Down).”

terms.definition: Entity as modeled by Active Inference , with Internal State separated from External State by Blanket State
terms.examples: “The ant nestmate is the Agent in the Active InferAnts model”
terms.connections: Agent has Agency

terms.definition: An Agent modifying the weights of its Internal State for the purpose of Action Planning and/or Belief updating . (This is a @realistCounterpart of Goal-driven selection .)
terms.examples: BacillisAxelis345 engaged in Cognition to decide whether to eat or to escape.

terms.definition: Group of more than one Agent.
terms.examples: The ant colony from a Behavioral, orCollective behavior perspective, is an Ensemble of nestmates.
terms.connections: Can have Group Renormalization Theory aspect or Stigmergy or Swarm

terms.definition: A formalism that describes the mapping between Hidden State, and Expectations of Action Prediction , Sensory outcome .
terms.definition2: Recognition Model Update Internal State parameter that correspond to External State (including Hidden State causes of environment states), Blanket State , and Internal State (meta-modeling). In contrast, Generative Model take those same Internal State parameter Estimator and emit expected or plausible observations.
terms.examples: Partially Observed Markov Decision Processes are commonly used for computational modeling of Generative Models.

terms.definition: Underlying @dynamical process in the Niche giving rise to Agent Observation and @agent Action Prediction
terms.definition2: Enactive ecological process using morphological computing processes where the Niche Regime of Attention @morphogenesis and generative model interact to create an embodied learning dyanamic.
terms.examples: The Generative Process generates Sensory Data.
terms.connections: The Generative Process exists in certain States that are Hidden State from the perspective of the Agent. When in a state, from the statistical perspective, it can generate a Sensory outcome which is sensed by the Agent. The agent will use this Sensory Data to form a Generative Model which may or may not match the true Generative Process but will nonetheless allow it to predict future outcomes drawn from the Generative Process if it has a good Generative Model.

terms.definition: Ecology System constituting the Generative Process (as Partitioned from the Agent who instantiates a Generative Model ).
terms.examples: Every ant lives in their ecological Niche.

terms.definition: Technically, a Non-Equilibrium Steady State requires a solution to the Fokker Planck equation (i.e., density dynamics). A nonequilibrium steady-state solution entails solenoidal (i.e., conservative or divergence free) dynamics that break detailed balance (and underwrite stochastic chaos ). In other words, The dynamics of systems at Non-Equilibrium Steady State are not time reversible (unlike equilibrium steady states, in which the flow is entirely dissipative).
terms.definition2: Generally, a Non-Equilibrium Steady State refers to a System with dynamics that are unchanging, or at Stationarity in some State.
terms.examples: Blood sugar levels are dynamic and fluctuating, however they revisit characteristic states repeatedly, this can be described by a Non-Equilibrium Steady State.
terms.connections: Similar to Stationarity

terms.definition: An Agent consisting of Blanket State and Internal State , partitioned off from Niche .
terms.examples: Internal State and Blanket States together constitute the Particle.

terms.definition: Recognition Model is the kind of Model that affords Variational Inference, which lets us calculate or approximate a probability distribution. Recognition Model is a synonym for Variational Model.
terms.definition2: In Dayan and Abbot (2001), the probability of a Hidden State (causes) given Sensory Data (effects) under some parameter.
terms.examples: After constructing a Generative Model, an Agent can invert this model to obtain the Recognition Model which allows for the prediction of the Hidden State (causes) that generated some new Sensory Data.

terms.definition: A structural correspondence between some Random variable inside a System and some Random variable outside the System (isomorphism being the strongest kind of correspondence), such that the Systemengages in Inference carried out by the System maintains the correspondence
terms.examples: The sterotypical neural pattern induced by a Stimulus is considered a Representation, at least by those who subscribe to Representationalism.

terms.definition: Broad sense: Generative Model that is aware of some Stimulus, reflected by its Salience
terms.definition2: Narrow sense: Attention modulates the the confidence on the Precision of Sense State, reflecting Sensory input
terms.examples: The Model instantly updated to the new Sensory Data because it was paying maximal Attention to the Stimulus.
terms.connections: Attention is structured in complex sociocultural Systems to realize a Regime of Attention

terms.definition: Data as recognized and interpreted by Generative Model of Agent
terms.examples: Every photon is like a piece of Evidence on the retina.

terms.definition: The Belief updating of an Internal State registered by a Sensory input, given the weighting assigned to that class of input in comparison with weighting of the competing Priors. (This is a narrow sense of “observation,” where the Agent is “looking for this kind of input.” This sense excludes situations where (a) an incoming stimulus with these attributes has already been explained-away or pre-discounted, or (b) the prior is so strongly weighted as to exclude updating in response to any inputs (other than, perhaps, “catastrophic” ones, as may occur in e.g. fainting, hysterical blindness).)
terms.definition2: Any Sensory input, either discrete-valued or continuous-valued. (This is a broad sense.)
terms.examples: One Observation can make all the difference.

terms.definition: Posterior State Inference after each new Observation.
terms.examples: Visual Perception gives us many demonstrations of the characteristics of our Generative Model — for example saccades, the blind spot, and blink supression.

terms.definition: The extent to which a Cue commands the Attention of an Agent given their Regime of Attention
terms.examples: Salience is related to how relevant a given Stimulus appears to be.
terms.connections: Related to Attention, for example of Affordance

terms.definition: A hierarchy of Estimators, which operate at different spatiotemporal timescales (so they track features at different scales); all carrying out Predictive Processing
terms.examples: A Nested Model is a Hierarchical Model, for example the Hierarchically Mechanistic Mind.

terms.definition: Agent engaged in Autopoiesis
terms.examples: A body is a Living system.

terms.definition: Realism framing of Hierarchical Model
terms.examples: The Generative Model of counties within states within countries, was a Multi-scale system

terms.definition: Set of relations described by State space of a Model .
terms.definition2: Differentiable and Integratable in terms of Variables and functions.
terms.examples: George Mobus argues that Systems have some fundamental properties such as structure and function.

terms.definition: (abstractCounterpart ?AB ?PHYS) relates a Physical entity to an Abstract one which is an idealized model in some dimension of the Physical entity.
terms.definition2: Example: an Abstract GraphNode could be stated to be the counterpart of an actual Computer in a ComputerNetwork.

terms.term: Action and Planning as Divergence Minimization

terms.definition: refers to the process of visual perceptions, in terms of oculomotor Sensorimotor Behavior and Cognitive System Generative Model
terms.definition2: refers to regime of visual perceptions through dynamical perturbations of light over the retina. As if feeling the textured light reflected off the niche surfaces
terms.examples: Active Vision
terms.connections: Epistemic value -driven Learning or Foraging

terms.definition: Phenomena of a System that recapitulates the material and informational causes of its own composition/existence.
terms.examples: In the right niche, cells can be considered to exhibit Autopoiesis at the System level.

terms.definition: Within Active Inference , this is the same as Policy selection
Ecology, Evolution, Development

One of two roads (arguments) that lead to the Free Energy Principle as a possible conclusion which starts with philosophical questions about what properties a thing must have to “exist” (i.e. it must be measurable) and then uses principles of Autopoiesis and non-equilibrium Thermodynamic systems from a statistical perspective to show what kinds of systems could continue to maintain themselves over time (see Friston 2019: Beyond the Desert Landscape and the other road, the Low road ). “The high road stands in for a top-down approach that starts by asking fundamental questions about the necessary properties things must possess if they exist. Using mathematical (variational) principles, once can then show that existence is an embodied exchange of a creatures with its environment - that necessarily entails predictive processing as one aspect of self-evidencing mechanics.”
terms.examples: The High road to the Free Energy Principle starts by talking about random Non-linear dynamical systems in general without a specific focus on biological organisms with brains.
terms.connections: The High road connects a number of different fields together, drawing from concepts of Autopoiesis and self-organization within random Non-linear dynamical systems who are proposed to be in Non-Equilibrium Steady State with respect to the environment. Under certain Ergodicity assumptions about such systems it is possible to show how the Free Energy Principle arises.

terms.definition: One of two roads (arguments) that lead to the Free Energy Principle as a possible conclusion which starts with fundamental questions from neuroscience and psychology about the nature of perception in biological organisms within a changing environment (see Friston 2019: Beyond the Desert Landscape and the other road, the High road ). “The low road is to pursue the agenda established by Kant and Helmholtz to generalize - in a bottom up way - the capacity for inference and prediction to see how far it takes us in understand embodied exchange with the environment.”
terms.examples: The Low road to the Free Energy Principle starts by looking at how biological organisms perceive their environment and take actions within it to develop a notion about how they can successfully predict the next state they will be in (Perception as Hypothesis testing).

terms.definition: Markovian Monism
Model Inversion
Principle
Realism
Risk
being ‘‘responsive to sensory impressions’’ through adaptive internal processes

a Stimulus , event, object , or Guidance signal that serves to guide Behavior , such as a retrieval cue, or that acts as a @Signal to the presentation of another stimulus, event, or object, such as an unconditioned stimulus or reinforcement. (dictionary.apa.org)
terms.connections: Approach to discussing Symbol in the Niche and social Culture patterns.

terms.definition: A Statistical manifold each of whose points corresponds to a Probability distribution (e.g. the expectation and variance of a normal density).
Inference on current and future Expectation of Action
terms.examples: The Generative Model over the next few timesteps with respect to Active States , is the Action Prediction .
terms.connections: Action Prediction is an enabling factor for Action Planning and Policy selection

terms.definition: A hypothesis or Model proposal for how a Principle is realized (e.g. the Free Energy Principle)
terms.definition2: A Model of the dynamics of a Thing, one that describes the process of development and change.
terms.examples: Friston in 2018 wrote “The distinction is between a State [theory] and Process Theory ; i.e., the difference between a normative principle that things may or may not conform to, and a Process Theory or hypothesis about how that principle is realized”

terms.definition: Biologically: Said of the behavior of a Neuronal Ensemble that minimizes the error of quantities selected by that ensemble, by implementing an algorithm that approximates calculus of variations.
terms.definition2: Mathematically: Using calculus of variationsto find functions that optimize the values of quantities that depend on those functions.

terms.definition: Unobserved variable in Bayesian Inference , can reflect a Latent cause .
Culture is the Niche for social Agent, that structures their Regime of Attention
Information used by Agent in context of entity- and event-oriented Cognition, specifically Hierarchical Model (Hierarchically Mechanistic Mind ).

The Internal State assumed by an Agent‘s epistemic Affordance, when unable to immediately (e.g. locally) resolve Uncertainty about the contingencies — i.e. the opportunity to resolve Uncertainty about ‘what would happen if I did that?’ (The Precision of this assumed Internal State has a distinctive, e.g. multimodal, distribution, i.e. exhibits Ambiguity .)

feedback mechanisms among practices in a Culture of scaffolding individuals’ Attention, that guide Agents’ style of Attention; act as determined by bodily, language, and contextual Cues in a given community; and are encoded in higher levels of the cortical hierarchy.
For no cost.
terms.definition2: Able to be liberated.

terms.definition: Synonym for Kullback-Leibler Divergence
terms.definition2: relative entropy

terms.definition: SUMO relation (represents ?THING ?ENTITY) means that ?THING in some way indicates, expresses, connotes, pictures, describes, etc. ?ENTITY. The Predicates containsInformation and realization are subrelations of represents.

nodes.label: Accuracy
nodes.meta: Broad sense: how “close to the mark” an Estimator is.

nodes.label: Ambiguity
nodes.meta: Broad sense: Extent to which stimuli have multiple plausible interpretations, requiring priors &/or Action for disambiguation

nodes.label: Bayesian Inference
nodes.meta: As opposed to frequentist analysis, Bayesian Inference uses a specified Prior or Empirical prior to Update the distributional Posterior

nodes.label: Belief
nodes.meta: Broad sense: Felt sense by an Agent of something being true, or confidence it is the case.

nodes.label: Belief updating
nodes.meta: Belief updating is changes in a Bayesian Inference Belief through time.

nodes.label: Complexity
nodes.meta: The extent to which an Agent must revise a Belief to explain incoming Sensory observations.

nodes.label: Data
nodes.meta: Data are a set of values of qualitative or quantitative variables about one or more Agent or object .

nodes.label: Expectation
nodes.meta: Within a Bayesian Inference framework, Expectation is an Estimator about future timesteps

nodes.label: Inference
nodes.meta: Process of reaching a (local or global) conclusion within a Model, for example with Bayesian Inference.

nodes.label: Information
nodes.meta: Measured in bits, the reduction of Uncertainty on a Belief distribution of some type. Usually Syntactic (Shannon) but also can be Semantic (e.g. Bayesian ).

nodes.label: Learning
nodes.meta: Broad sense: Process of an Agent engaged in Updates to Cognition (and possibly) Behavior.

nodes.label: Outcome
nodes.meta: If we consider the environment as a Generative Process that can be sampled when in a particular state, the statistical result (Data, Sensory observation) of the…

nodes.label: Posterior
nodes.meta: The Update to the Prior after Observation has occurred

nodes.label: Prediction
nodes.meta: An Estimator about a State in a Model at a future time.

nodes.label: Prior
nodes.meta: The initial or preceding state of a Belief in Bayesian Inference, before Sensory Data (Observation or Evidence ) occurs.

nodes.label: State
nodes.meta: is the statistical, computational, or mathematical value for a parameter within the State space of a Model .

nodes.label: State space
nodes.meta: Set of variables/parameters that describe a System .

nodes.label: Stationarity
nodes.meta: Of a Random variable , that it is described by parameters that are drawn from a Gaussian distribution and unchanging over the time horizon of analysis.

nodes.label: Surprise
nodes.meta: In Bayesian Inference, Surprise is the negative log evidence, directly corresponding to the inverse of probability (high probability, low surprisal; low probabi…

nodes.label: Temporal Depth
nodes.meta: The length of a time window or horizon considered (longer time → deeper / more Temporal Depth )

nodes.label: Uncertainty
nodes.meta: In Bayesian Inference , a measure of the Expectation of Surprise (Entropy) of a Random variable (associated with its variance or inverse Precision )

nodes.label: Action
nodes.meta: Broad sense: The dynamics, mechanisms, and measurements of Behavior

nodes.label: Action Planning
nodes.meta: The selection of an Affordance based upon Inference of Expected Free Energy

nodes.label: Agency
nodes.meta: The ability of an Agent to engage in Action in their Niche and enact Goal-driven selection or Policy selection based upon Preference

nodes.label: Behavior
nodes.meta: The sequence of Action that an Agent is observed to enact.

nodes.label: Policy
nodes.meta: Sequence of Actions, reflected by series of Active States as implemented in Policy selection which is Action Prediction or Action and Planning as Divergence Min…

nodes.label: Policy selection
nodes.meta: The process of an Agent engaging in Action Planning from set of Affordance, in Active Inference based upon minimization of Expected Free Energy

nodes.label: Preference
nodes.meta: parameter in Bayesian Inference Markov Decision Process that ranks or scores the extent to how an Agent values Sensory input .

nodes.label: Active Inference
nodes.meta: Active Inference is a Process Theory related to Free Energy Principle .

nodes.label: Epistemic value
nodes.meta: is the value of Information gain or Expectation of reduction in Uncertainty about a State with respect to a Policy, used in Policy selection

nodes.label: Expected Free Energy
nodes.meta: Measure for performing Inference on Action over a given time horizon (Policy selection , Action and Planning as Divergence Minimization ).

nodes.label: Free Energy
nodes.meta: Free Energy is an Information Theoretic quantity that constitutes an upper bound on Surprisal .

nodes.label: Free Energy Principle
nodes.meta: A generalization of Predictive Coding (PC) according to which organisms minimize an upper bound on the Entropy of Sensory input (or sensory signals) (the Free E…

nodes.label: Generalized Free Energy
nodes.meta: Past Variational Free Energy plus future Expected Free Energy (each totaled over Policy).

nodes.label: Pragmatic Value
nodes.meta: Pragmatic Value is the benefit to an organism of a given Policy or Action , measured in terms of probability of a Policy leading to Expectation of Random variab…

nodes.label: Variational Free Energy
nodes.meta: Measure that performs e.g. Inference on Sensory Data given a Generative Model , F. The two sources that composeVariational Free Energy are Model overfitting and…

nodes.label: Active States
nodes.meta: In the Friston Blanket formalism, the Blanket State are the Sense State (incoming Sensory input) and Active States (outgoing influence of Policy selection )

nodes.label: Blanket State
nodes.meta: Set of states in the Markov Blanket Partition that make Internal State and External State have Conditional Probability that are independent.

nodes.label: External State
nodes.meta: States with Conditional density independent from Internal State, conditioned on Blanket State.

nodes.label: Friston Blanket
nodes.meta: Markov Blanket with partitioned Active States and Sense State .

nodes.label: Internal State
nodes.meta: States with Conditional density independent from External State , conditioned on Blanket State.

nodes.label: Markov Blanket
nodes.meta: Markov Partitioning Model of System, reflecting Agent as delineated from the Niche via an Interface. The Markov Blanket Blanket State reflect the State(s) upon …

nodes.label: Markov Decision Process
nodes.meta: Bayesian Inference Model where Agent Generative Model can implement Policy selection on Affordances reflected by Active States, while other features of the Gene…

nodes.label: Sense State
nodes.meta: In the Friston Blanket formalism, the Blanket State are the Sense State (incoming Sensory input) and Active States (outgoing influence of Policy selection )

nodes.label: Affordance
nodes.meta: Options or capacities for Action by an Agent (sometimes called “Affordance 3.0”)

nodes.label: Agent
nodes.meta: Entity as modeled by Active Inference , with Internal State separated from External State by Blanket State

nodes.label: Cognition
nodes.meta: An Agent modifying the weights of its Internal State for the purpose of Action Planning and/or Belief updating . (This is a @realistCounterpart of Goal-driven s…

nodes.label: Ensemble
nodes.meta: Group of more than one Agent.

nodes.label: Generative Model
nodes.meta: A formalism that describes the mapping between Hidden State, and Expectations of Action Prediction , Sensory outcome .

nodes.label: Generative Process
nodes.meta: Underlying @dynamical process in the Niche giving rise to Agent Observation and @agent Action Prediction

nodes.label: Niche
nodes.meta: Ecology System constituting the Generative Process (as Partitioned from the Agent who instantiates a Generative Model ).

nodes.label: Non-Equilibrium Steady State
nodes.meta: Technically, a Non-Equilibrium Steady State requires a solution to the Fokker Planck equation (i.e., density dynamics). A nonequilibrium steady-state solution e…

nodes.label: Particle
nodes.meta: An Agent consisting of Blanket State and Internal State , partitioned off from Niche .

nodes.label: Recognition Model
nodes.meta: Recognition Model is the kind of Model that affords Variational Inference, which lets us calculate or approximate a probability distribution. Recognition Model …

nodes.label: Representation
nodes.meta: A structural correspondence between some Random variable inside a System and some Random variable outside the System (isomorphism being the strongest kind of co…

nodes.label: Attention
nodes.meta: Broad sense: Generative Model that is aware of some Stimulus, reflected by its Salience

nodes.label: Evidence
nodes.meta: Data as recognized and interpreted by Generative Model of Agent

nodes.label: Observation
nodes.meta: The Belief updating of an Internal State registered by a Sensory input, given the weighting assigned to that class of input in comparison with weighting of the …

nodes.label: Perception
nodes.meta: Posterior State Inference after each new Observation.

nodes.label: Salience
nodes.meta: The extent to which a Cue commands the Attention of an Agent given their Regime of Attention

nodes.label: Hierarchical Model
nodes.meta: A hierarchy of Estimators, which operate at different spatiotemporal timescales (so they track features at different scales); all carrying out Predictive Proces…

nodes.label: Living system
nodes.meta: Agent engaged in Autopoiesis

nodes.label: Multi-scale system
nodes.meta: Realism framing of Hierarchical Model

nodes.label: System
nodes.meta: Set of relations described by State space of a Model .

nodes.label: abstractCounterpart
nodes.meta: (abstractCounterpart ?AB ?PHYS) relates a Physical entity to an Abstract one which is an idealized model in some dimension of the Physical entity.

nodes.label: Action and Planning as Divergence Minimization
Active Vision
nodes.meta: refers to the process of visual perceptions, in terms of oculomotor Sensorimotor Behavior and Cognitive System Generative Model

nodes.label: Autopoiesis
nodes.meta: Phenomena of a System that recapitulates the material and informational causes of its own composition/existence.

nodes.label: Decision-making
nodes.meta: Within Active Inference , this is the same as Policy selection

nodes.label: EcoEvoDevo
nodes.meta: Ecology, Evolution, Development

nodes.label: High road
nodes.meta: One of two roads (arguments) that lead to the Free Energy Principle as a possible conclusion which starts with philosophical questions about what properties a t…

nodes.label: Low road
nodes.meta: One of two roads (arguments) that lead to the Free Energy Principle as a possible conclusion which starts with fundamental questions from neuroscience and psych…

nodes.label: Markovian Monism
nodes.meta: Markovian Monism

nodes.label: Model Inversion
nodes.meta: Model Inversion

nodes.label: Principle
nodes.meta: Principle

nodes.label: Realism
nodes.meta: Realism

nodes.label: Risk
nodes.meta: Risk

nodes.label: Sentience
nodes.meta: being ‘‘responsive to sensory impressions’’ through adaptive internal processes

nodes.label: Cue
nodes.meta: a Stimulus , event, object , or Guidance signal that serves to guide Behavior , such as a retrieval cue, or that acts as a @Signal to the presentation of anothe…

nodes.label: Information Geometry
nodes.meta: A Statistical manifold each of whose points corresponds to a Probability distribution (e.g. the expectation and variance of a normal density).

nodes.label: Action Prediction
nodes.meta: Inference on current and future Expectation of Action

nodes.label: Process Theory
nodes.meta: A hypothesis or Model proposal for how a Principle is realized (e.g. the Free Energy Principle)

nodes.label: Variational
nodes.meta: Biologically: Said of the behavior of a Neuronal Ensemble that minimizes the error of quantities selected by that ensemble, by implementing an algorithm that ap…

nodes.label: Hidden State
nodes.meta: Unobserved variable in Bayesian Inference , can reflect a Latent cause .

nodes.label: Culture
nodes.meta: Culture is the Niche for social Agent, that structures their Regime of Attention

nodes.label: Narrative
nodes.meta: Information used by Agent in context of entity- and event-oriented Cognition, specifically Hierarchical Model (Hierarchically Mechanistic Mind ).

nodes.label: Novelty
nodes.meta: The Internal State assumed by an Agent‘s epistemic Affordance, when unable to immediately (e.g. locally) resolve Uncertainty about the contingencies — i.e. the …

nodes.label: Regime of Attention
nodes.meta: feedback mechanisms among practices in a Culture of scaffolding individuals’ Attention, that guide Agents’ style of Attention; act as determined by bodily, lang…

nodes.label: free
nodes.meta: For no cost.

nodes.label: relative entropy
nodes.meta: Synonym for Kullback-Leibler Divergence

nodes.label: represents
nodes.meta: SUMO relation (represents ?THING ?ENTITY) means that ?THING in some way indicates, expresses, connotes, pictures, describes, etc. ?ENTITY. The Predicates contai…
